input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system under study is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes designed for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining. Key components include a permanent magnet synchronous motor, gear transmission, and cycloidal gear in the first vertical robot axis, controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers services including continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction to enhance motion accuracy, real-time integrated visualization via a unified dashboard, and semantic support for comprehensive system understanding. These capabilities target manufacturing stakeholders, enabling precise monitoring, predictive maintenance, and optimization of robotic system performance.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT; architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector modeling; behavior modeling via MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines; simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink; and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java) for detailed behavior implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system architecture is defined by a Digital Shadow Reference Model structured as a UML class diagram, specifying core concepts such as data traces, processing components, and metadata. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) including SysML, AADL, MontiArc, and ACME are employed to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Concretization of the reference model involves creating conforming class diagrams that map reference concepts to concrete elements, enabling precise traceability of data processing steps through 'originates from' associations and transitive traceability for computed data traces.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems utilizes Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized interoperability and MQTT protocol for real-time sensor data streaming, ensuring seamless data exchange between physical assets and digital components while adhering to industry-specific communication standards.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML across requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer abstractions. Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs define structural components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling utilizes MontiArc Automaton and SysML v1.7 state machines, while analytical models are implemented using MathWorks Simulink, other simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java). Data management integrates heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives and modeling notations as per [31].
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical to digital via an automated one-way stream of sensor time series data using the MQTT protocol, as explicitly stated in Document 1: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT'.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication protocol is MQTT for sensor data transmission; no details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security protocols are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle stages involve Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) during development, with modeling split into requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer abstractions. Methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT are employed to manage complexity across these stages, integrating heterogeneous stakeholder perspectives through systems engineering modeling languages like SysML.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process employs a purpose-driven methodology from reference [15] for creating digital shadows, applied and refined in the use case. This includes data aggregation and abstraction for specific purposes, utilizing simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink, C++, and Java for behavior modeling, and architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component & connector perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system derives friction parameters (static, kinetic, viscous) from gearbox data to improve robot control accuracy, with insights generated through data aggregation and visualization in a unified dashboard for integrated decision support.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system utilizes SysML for systems engineering modeling, adhering to the SysML standard [17], and employs architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME [23], AADL [24], and MontiArc [25, 26] for component & connector-based system descriptions. It integrates methodologies including RFLP [18, 19], OOSEM [20], SPES [21], and SMArDT [22] to manage system complexity across requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers, ensuring compliance with industry modeling standards and interoperability between heterogeneous tools and stakeholders.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the specific robot model (MABI MAX 100), its configuration (six axes), and its primary application (machining). Supported by quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor... gear transmission... cycloidal gear', 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl.'

The description correctly identifies the core services: multi-physics-aware friction identification, visualization via a dashboard, and semantic support for system understanding. However, it includes 'predictive maintenance' as a capability, which is not explicitly mentioned in the source text, where the focus is on improving motion accuracy. Supported by quotes: 'continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction', 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard', 'lead to semantic support'.

The extraction is highly detailed and matches Section II.A perfectly. The text lists MBSE methodologies like 'RFLP', 'OOSEM', 'SPES', or 'SMArDT'. It identifies ADLs such as 'ACME', 'AADL', 'MontiArc', or 'SysML IBDs' for component modeling. It mentions behavior modeling via 'MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML v1.7' and simulation tools like 'MathWorks Simulink' and 'code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'. Section IV confirms the use of 'SysML model' and 'MathWorks MATLAB Simulink' in the implementation.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the one-way data flow and the specific protocol used. It is strongly grounded in the source text, which defines the digital shadow's communication flow and the specific sensor data transmission method. Supported by quotes: 'digital shadow is characterized by “an automated one-way data flow”' and 'time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT'.

The text defines the system as a Digital Shadow, which is characterized as 'a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system'. Since the system does not support this type of interaction, there is no description of control flows, message formats, or validation mechanisms to extract. The extraction is correct.

The extraction correctly identifies 'MQTT' as the communication protocol based on the quote 'streamed via MQTT'. It also accurately notes that other technical network details such as topology or bandwidth are not mentioned in the text.

The source text describes the modeling (SysML, Simulink) and logic of the digital shadow but does not contain any information regarding hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational or storage requirements, or scalability mechanisms. The extraction correctly identifies that this information is missing.

Supported by quotes: 'derive the friction (static, kinetic, and viscous) within the gearbox of the robot’s first axis', 'improve the robot control’s accuracy', 'integrated visualization within a unified dashboard'.

The description accurately reflects the source document's content about standardization. It correctly mentions the use of SysML ('Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML [17]'), various ADLs ('ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML IBDs [17], [27]'), and methodologies ('RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20], SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]') to manage system complexity. The description is highly specific and detailed, providing in-depth technical explanation of the standardization approaches.
